_note: Form codes defined in IEC 61439-2 Annex 101. ASCII diagrams use: BUSBARS at top, FU = functional unit compartment, T = outgoing-terminal compartment, | = metallic barrier, space = same compartment.

forms.title: Form 1 — no internal segregation
ascii_diagram: |  BUSBARS  FU  FU  FU  T  T  T   |
applications: Lowest-cost option where whole-board de-energising is acceptable for any maintenance
disadvantages: No live-work safety — entire board is exposed once opened

vs_neighbours.Form_2a: 2a adds a barrier between busbar and functional units

forms.usage_notes: Acceptable only for plant-only assemblies where maintenance is always with the whole board off.

forms.title: Form 2a — busbars segregated, functional units in common compartment, terminals not segregated from busbar
ascii_diagram: |  FU  FU  FU  T  T  T            |
applications: Industrial sub-distribution above 250 A where busbar isolation is needed
advantages: Busbar can stay live while functional units are de-energised
disadvantages: Outgoing terminations remain at busbar potential when busbar is live

vs_neighbours.Form_2b: 2b adds segregation of outgoing terminals from busbar

forms.usage_notes: Entry-level form for installations needing busbar isolation.

forms.title: Form 2b — busbars segregated, functional units common, outgoing terminals segregated from busbar
advantages: Can work on outgoing terminations without de-energising busbar
disadvantages: Functional units still share a compartment
forms.usage_notes: Common default for commercial mains 400–1600 A where critical-circuit independence is not required.

forms.title: Form 3a — busbar and functional-unit segregation, terminals not segregated from busbar
ascii_diagram: | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU |   |
|  T    T    T    T    T    T     |
applications: Industrial MCCs where outgoing terminations remain in busbar compartment
advantages: Single functional unit can be worked on while neighbours stay live
forms.usage_notes: Industrial-only — Form 3b is preferred for new commercial designs.

forms.title: Form 3b — busbar and functional-unit segregation, terminals segregated from busbar but grouped per unit
ascii_diagram: | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU |   |
advantages: Single circuit maintenance with rest live

vs_neighbours.Form_2b: Adds segregation between functional units (was a single bay)
vs_neighbours.Form_3a: Form 3a has outgoing terminals in the SAME compartment as the functional unit; 3b separates them
vs_neighbours.Form_4a: Form 4a separates each outgoing terminal individually rather than as a group

forms.usage_notes: Default choice for commercial main switchboards 400–4000 A. Specify explicitly on the assembly schedule — the OEM cannot infer the Form from a generic 'main switchboard' callout.

forms.title: Form 4a — busbar, functional-unit, and grouped-terminal separation
ascii_diagram: | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU |   |
advantages: Each functional unit's terminations isolated from neighbours'

vs_neighbours.Form_4b: 4b adds individual-terminal separation within a unit

forms.title: Form 4b — busbar, functional-unit, and individual-terminal separation
ascii_diagram: | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU | FU |   |
advantages: Highest practical segregation; each outgoing terminal in own compartment

vs_neighbours.Form_4a: Adds separation between individual terminals within the same functional unit

forms.usage_notes: Specify only where regulatory/operational availability requirement justifies the cost premium.
